JavaScript + HTTP
  • Dice
  • GPS
  • Grocery List
  • Bitcoin
  • Divvy Bikes

Variables and Objects

  • Use let or const to define variables.
  • Arrays use [ ] brackets.
  • Objects are key-value containers and use { } braces.
  • Try using the window object to pop up an alert.
  • Watch out for type conversions! Use === instead of ==.

Modifying the DOM

  • querySelector() function to get a single element
  • querySelectorAll() function to get an array of matching elements
  • Use a for...of loop to iterate over an array.
  • Be sure to wait for the DOMContentLoaded event on the document object.

Events

  • Use addEventListener() to handle a specific event on a specific DOM element.
  • Experiment with monitorEvents() in the Console to see what's possible.
  • Challenge 1: Make an entire section of this page disappear when you click on a blue section heading.
  • Challenge 2: When you click here here to go to Apple.com, show an alert box instead.

Built-In Methods and Properties

  • Play with the Chrome Console to get a feel for what's possible.
  • Global objects
    • window
    • document
    • console
    • navigator
  • window.querySelectorAll() will return an array of all elements matching the given CSS selector expression.
  • element.style
  • element.parentElement
  • element.textContent
  • element.innerHTML
  • window.alert(...)
  • window.confirm(...)
  • console.debug(...) and console.log(...) are synonymous
  • console.warn(...)
  • console.error(...)

Custom Objects 


let monster = { name: "Cookie Monster",
           favorite_color: "Blue",
           favorite_fruit: "Cookies",
           speak: function() { alert("Me want cookie!"); }
};

monster.speak();

Cheat Sheet for Week 3

  • This week, the images subfolder contains images of dice.
  • Use let or const to define variables.
  • Define functions by assigning them to variable names, like this: 
    
let f = function(arg1, arg2) {
// code goes here
}
  • Given an element elem, you can attach a "click" event handler with: elem.addEventListener("click", myFunctionName)
  • Your event handler functions should accept one argument, which is the event object: 
    
let myHandler = function(event_obj) { ... }
  • To prevent the browser from also responding to a click event, you can call event_obj.preventDefault();
  • When inside an event handler function, you can refer to the original element by using event.target.
  • To reserve a blank section of the page for your JavaScript to add HTML later, use an empty div and give it an id attribute so your code can find it: 
    
<div id="mydiv"></div>
  • Generate a random integer from 1 to 10 with: Math.floor(Math.random() * 10) + 1
